We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accounting Office to:
Effect the necessary adjustments to correct the reported balances of affected accounts in the FS. Accordingly, working paper adjustment entries shall be prepared for the restatement of the affected accounts to present the correct CY 2024 restated figures in the preparation of the CY 2025 comparative FS; and
Perform year-end cash cut-off procedures to ensure that cash transactions are recorded in the correct accounting period.
Instruct the Municipal Treasury Office to:
Review the check register for any unreleased checks as of year-end, prepare a “Schedule of Unreleased Checks” accordingly and furnish a copy to the Municipal Accounting Office; and
Determine whether the dormant bank account is still necessary. If so, request activation of the account. Otherwise, revert the account balance to PhilHealth or the Bureau of the Treasury, whichever is practicable and close the account thereafter.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustments on the misclassification of accounts by debiting “Loans Receivable-Others” and crediting “Other Receivable” in the amount of ₱0.185 million. Accordingly, working paper adjustment entries shall be prepared for the restatement of the affected accounts to present the correct CY 2024 restated figures in the preparation of the CY 2025 comparative FSs;
Direct the Accounting Office to record an allowance for impairment on the adjusted balance of the “Loans Receivable-Others” account with the following entries under the TF:
Demand from the concerned official the immediate and full liquidation of the outstanding cash advances aged below ten years; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly review and monitor the status of the receivable accounts and observe the guidelines and procedures in the proper disposition of dormant accounts as prescribed in COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management:
Reorganize the Inventory Committee by clearly designating a chairperson and members, along with their respective roles and responsibilities;
Direct the Inventory Committee, in coordination with the Municipal Accounting Office and COA, to immediately conduct a complete physical inventory of its PPE, document the results in the prescribed RPCPPE and reconcile it with the accounting records. Moving forward, the Committee shall ensure that a physical count is conducted annually and that the RPCPPE is submitted to the Office of the Auditor no later than January 31 of the following year; and
Require the Property Custodian and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately establish and regularly update Property Cards for each PPE category based on the results of the physical count and ensure that each item is tagged with a property number and properly tracked using these records for accountability and monitoring purposes.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer to:
Immediately conduct reconciliation of records and assess the actual completion of the projects recorded in the CIP accounts. Relative to this, the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Engineer and Agency's Inspectorate Team shall conduct an inventory on all the ongoing projects and reconcile the differences noted in the records of the two offices; and
Exert efforts to identify the projects with lacking details, determine its commencement, completion and status. Otherwise, include these items in the one-time cleansing of PPE balances following the procedures and guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
Advise the Municipal Engineer to issue Certificate of Completion after careful evaluation and inspection of each successfully completed project and transmit the same, together with other integral documents evidencing to the Accounting Office for proper recording;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Reclassify, based on sufficient documentation, the fully completed projects from CIP to the appropriate PPE accounts. Also, develop and enforce internal controls and monitoring mechanisms to ensure timely reclassification of completed projects from CIP to PPE and proper recognition of obligations to contractors and suppliers; and
Reclassify the ₱2.068 million from CIP to Advances to Contractors. Moreover, strict adherence to the provisions of the GAM should be observed in future transactions to ensure proper recording, transparency, and faithful representation of financial transactions.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Inventory Committee together with the Municipal Agriculturist to conduct the physical count and reappraisal of breeding stocks simultaneously, then submit the report to the Accounting Office and the Office of the Auditor; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to create SL cards for the breeding stocks based on the result of the physical count and reappraisal, prepare the necessary adjusting entries, as warranted and submit the JEVs to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the appropriate journal entries relative to the unrecorded liabilities totaling ₱1.656 million. Accordingly, working paper adjustment entries shall be prepared for the restatement of the affected accounts to present the correct CY 2024 restated figures in the preparation of the CY 2025 comparative financial statements;
Ensure that goods and services that have been delivered or rendered as at year end be properly recorded as liabilities. Relative to this, require, as year-end cut-off procedures, the Municipal General Services Office to forward to the Municipal Accounting Office all claims for delivered goods and services for the proper recognition of Accounts Payable on the related transactions; and
Investigate the nature and validity of the ₱96,210.00 long outstanding “Accounts Payable”. Relative to this, Management shall coordinate with the identified supplier. The residual amount after reconciliation shall be reverted to the unappropriated surplus.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Conduct a comprehensive review of the existing balances of the accounts “Due to NGAs” and “Due to LGUs,” and reconstruct a detailed Monitoring Report and/or SL, disaggregated by specific agency, transaction, and fund source, to ensure traceability, accuracy, and completeness of recorded liabilities; and
Establish and implement a regular monitoring and reconciliation process between the GL and SL to validate the existence, accuracy, and aging of outstanding payables, and to promptly identify and resolve any discrepancies.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the PGB to determine the validity and details such as scope of works, specific buildings to be repaired, among others, on the funds received for the repair of Itbayat Central School;
If the fund can still be utilized by the Agency, direct the concerned offices to facilitate the implementation of the project with due; and
Provide adequate disclosures in the NFS regarding the sources and purposes of the items Trust Liabilities.

Conduct a thorough review on the outstanding Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable account to determine the nature and validity of the balances;
Establish and implement regular monitoring and reconciliation procedures to promptly identify and address long-outstanding balances; and

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to:

The shares of the Barangays of Ibayat and the PGB to the “Due to LGUs” account;

We recommended that Management:
require the Agency’s Inventory Committee, Appraisal Committee in coordination with the Municipal Agriculturist to ascertain whether or not fair value can be determined for the existing biological assets of the Agency. If to the affirmative,  ascertain the fair value less cost to sell of the identified biological assets and prepare a report thereon to be submitted to the Accounting Office;
direct the Municipal Accountant, based on the report to be prepared by the concerned personnel, to prepare adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the “Breeding Stock” account. We recommended that Management provide an explanation/justification with verifiable basis why the payment of Economic Relief Assistance which was noted as non-compliant with existing laws and COA rules and regulations, as mentioned above, should not be disallowed in post-audit. Otherwise, require the immediate refund of the amount received by all payees.
Quoted below is the reply of the Municipal Vice Mayor duly noted by the Municipal Mayor in a letter dated April 19, 2022
“Pursuant to the provisions of the Local Government Code of 1991, it stipulates that the Sangguniang Bayan has an inherent power to enact local laws necessary, appropriate or incidental to the promotion of the general welfare of its constituents. It is for this reason that the Sangguniang Bayan of Itbayat enacted Ordinance No. 22-0360 granting Calamity Relief Assistance to Officials and Employees of the Municipal Government of Itbayat considering that the Municipal Officials and Employees were not spared from the damages and crisis brought about by the series of catastrophe that struck the island Municipality. This is manifested by Resolution No. 21-0321 and Resolution No. 21-0259 which declared the Municipality as under state of calamity due to prolonged drought, Covid-19 pandemic and devastations caused by Super Typhoon Kiko respectively.
In view of this, we respectfully appeal to consider the legislated move of the Sangguniang Bayan favorably which is mainly for humanitarian purposes.”
Auditor’s rejoinder: It is well-settled rule that conformity with law is one of the essential requisites for the validity of a municipal ordinance (The People of the Philippines v. Chong Hong et al.,). The Ordinance No. 21-113 authorizing the grant of ERA failed to conform with RA 6758 or  the Salary Standardization Law and Section 02 of Administrative Order No. 37 dated November 21, 1998 as discussed above.
In Madera, et al. vs. COA, G.R. No. 244128 dated September 8, 2020, the Court concurred with the view of the COA that the compensation-setting power in Section 447 of the Local Government Code to grant subject benefits cannot prevail over Section 12 of RA No. 6758 or the Salary Standardization Law. This case involves a Petition for Certiorari assailing the COA Decision which affirmed the Notices of Disallowances on benefits such as Economic Crisis Assistance (ECA), Monetary Augmentation of Municipal Agency (MAMA), Agricultural Crisis Assistance (ACA) and Mitigation Allowance to Municipal Employees (MAME) given to the officials and employees of Mondragon, Northern Samar. Finding no grave abuse of discretion on the part of the COA, the Court upheld the NDs against the subject allowances.
In view of the foregoing, we maintain that the Economic Relief Assistance for 2021 totaling P1,265,000.00  shall be disallowed for want of legal basis. Proper basis in law is lacking and there is no showing of clear, direct, and reasonable connection to the actual performance of the payee-recipient's official work and functions for which the benefit or incentive was intended as further compensation, thus, the return to government coffers of the amounts given as assistance is necessary.

We recommend that grant of professional fees to the Doctor to the Barrio in the succeeding months shall be withheld with the corresponding tax taking into consideration compliance of the documentary requirement in determining the applicable rate as stipulated in the provisions of Section 2.57.2 of RR No. 30-2003.
13.6 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the noted difference in the reciprocal accounts and effect necessary adjustments to reflect the correct account balance as of year-end.

14.9 We recommended that management revise the agency’s guidelines in the grant of SEA-K assistance that is in conformity with existing rules and regulations to ensure the economy, efficiency and effectiveness in the operation of government funds. Likewise, identification of recipients should be carefully looked into so that the viability of repayment will not be at stake.
14.10 We also recommend that all the necessary supporting documents must be submitted as requisites for the entitlement on the said program in conformity with Section 4.4 of COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 25, 2017.
15.4 We recommend that management exert extra effort to cause the collection of said receivables and to avail of administrative and legal remedies to effect collection.

16.6 We recommend management to direct the designated property officer to facilitate the conduct of physical count in order to come up with correct Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment.  Likewise, the accounting office must record all kinds of receipt of assets, liabilities and capital outlay in order to reflect correct balances in the financial statements. The report should be reconciled with the balances of the  account at the Municipal Accounting Office, copy furnished the Office of the Auditor.

17.4 We recommend that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer submit the Municipal accounts and financial reports within the prescribed period as required by the above-mentioned rules and regulations.
